data.title: Wacker Chemie to Raise Prices for Silicone Products Due to Middle East War
data.description:
By Aimee Look

Wacker Chemie said it will raise prices for its silicone-product range, as it contends with higher costs due to the conflict in the Middle East.

The German chemical company said in a statement Friday that it will increase prices at the start of April for its silicon-based products. Wacker said the war in the Middle East has caused distortions in the energy and raw materials worldwide, and a significant disruption in supply chains.

"Due to this and to considerable disruptions to international trade routes prices for oil, natural gas, raw materials and logistics have risen palpably," the company said. It will inform customers accordingly, Wacker said.

Wacker's price increase for silicone products follows a move it made last week to bump up prices for the product range of its polymers division, where it also cited increased costs due to the war. For polymers, the extent of the price adjustment will depend on where products are sourced and existing contractual agreements, the company said at the time.

Silicone products accounted for around 50% of the group's sales in 2025, with over 2,800 specialized products in the category, Wacker said Friday.

Write to Aimee Look at aimee.look@wsj.com

data.title: India approves $2.14 billion credit guarantee scheme for microfinance firms
data.description:
By Gopika Gopakumar and Ashwin Manikandan

India's finance ministry has approved a 200 billion rupee ($2.14 billion) credit guarantee scheme for microfinance firms to address mounting stress in the sector, a document issued by the National Credit Guarantee Trustee Company (NCGTC) showed on Friday.

The scheme, which went into effect on Friday and ends on June 30, aims to bolster credit flow to small- and medium-sized microfinance institutions (MFIs) and renew lenders' confidence in the sector.

Government-owned NCGTC will guarantee loans extended to MFIs under the scheme, with lenders required to cap interest at the external benchmark lending rate (EBLR), or 2% above the marginal cost of funds.

MFIs, in turn, will be required to lend at a rate 1% below the six-month average lending rate.

The loan guarantees will cover 70% of disbursals to large MFIs, 75% to medium-sized MFIs and 80% to smaller institutions. The maximum loan tenure has been set at three years, including a one-year moratorium and two years for repayment.

At least 5% of loans under the scheme are to be allocated to small MFIs and 10% to medium-sized MFIs.

Each loan extended to small MFIs will be capped at 1 billion rupees, medium MFIs at 2 billion rupees and large MFIs at 3 billion rupees. Total disbursals are to be limited to 20% of each lender's total loanbook.

MFI LENDING DOWN

The microfinance sector, which peaked in March 2024 with 4.3 trillion rupees in total loans outstanding, has since shrunk.

Total outstanding loans fell to 3.14 trillion rupees as of December 31 last year, down 7.3% from September 30, according to data from the Microfinance Institutions Network (MFIN).

"This scheme will play a critical role in unlocking liquidity, particularly for small and medium MFIs, and ensuring that affordable credit continues to reach low-income households," said Alok Misra, CEO of MFIN.

In June 2024, a deputy governor of India's central bank said the sector was grappling with high interest rates, rising borrower over-indebtedness and coercive recovery practices.

India had introduced a similar 75 billion rupee credit guarantee scheme for MFIs during the COVID-19 pandemic.

($1 = 93.5440 Indian rupees)

data.title: Wall Street brokerages continue to expect two Fed rate cuts by end-2026
data.description:
Major brokerages are still forecasting two U.S. Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts in 2026 in contrast to the central bank's latest projections, which signaled a single quarter-point reduction amid renewed inflation concerns fueled by the Middle East war.

Morgan Stanley became the latest brokerage to revise its forecast for the central bank, joining peers such as Goldman Sachs and Barclays to expect the first rate reduction in September, compared with June previously.

Earlier this week, the Fed left rates unchanged at its March meeting , as expected. Investors focused on a cautious tone by Fed Chair Jerome Powell - that the economic outlook remains uncertain amid a U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.

Interest rate futures suggest traders see little chance of interest-rate cuts before mid-2027, according to the CME's FedWatch tool.

data.title: Baltic Index flat amid mixed performance across vessel segments
data.description:
The Baltic Exchange's dry bulk freight index, which monitors rates for ships carrying dry bulk commodities, held almost steady on Friday, as small gains in the capesize segment were offset by minor losses in the panamax and supramax segments.

* The main Baltic index , which tracks rates for capesize, panamax and supramax vessels, fell 1 point to 2,056.

* The index gained 1.4% for the week.

* The capesize index (.BACI) added 6 points, or 0.2%, at 2,971, and logged a 3.2% weekly gain.

* Average daily earnings for capesize vessels (.BATCA), which typically transport 150,000-ton cargoes including iron ore and coal, increased $49 to $23,438.

* Iron ore futures gained as China's restrictions on buying miner BHP's products are expected to come into effect, while recovering hot metal output in the country supported demand.

* The panamax index (.BPNI) was down 5 points, or 0.3%, at 1,904, but was up 3.6% for the week.

* Average daily earnings for panamax vessels (.BPWT), which usually carry 60,000 to 70,000 tons of coal or grain, decreased $45 to $17,132.

* The supramax index (.BSIS) also fell 5 points, or 0.4%, to 1,224, and posted a weekly loss of 4.6%.

* Shipping and freight rates have been increasing due to the Middle East war-linked closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most critical oil artery.

data.title: Euro Credit Risk Premiums Rise as Middle East War Continues — Market Talk
data.description: The risk premium on euro-denominated corporate bonds climbs as investors take precaution amid the Middle East war. "The increase has been comparatively modest so far," LBBW credit analysts say in a note. However, an escalation in the conflict raises the risks for global economies and could lead to significant credit spread widening, the analysts say. Spreads on euro-denominated investment-grade bonds hit their highest in nearly nine months at 101.66 basis points on Thursday, according to the Iboxx euro corporates index.(miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Lightspeed Commerce: Payments Growth Can't Offset Software Stagnation
data.description:
Investment Thesis: Transaction Revenues Masking Structural Problems

Lightspeed Commerce has delivered impressive transaction-based revenue growthup 33% year-over-year in Q2 FY2026 to $216 millionas payments penetration reached 37%, up from 25% a year ago. The company just surpassed $1 billion in trailing twelve-month revenue and raised full-year FY2026 guidance to at least 12% growth. Management celebrated reaching positive adjusted EBITDA of $32 million on a TTM basis.

The market remains skeptical. Shares trade at $8.80 (as of March 2026), down 24% since the beginning of 2026 and 92% from the 2021 peak of $123. The fundamental problem: Lightspeed is shifting toward lower-margin transaction revenues (29% gross margin) while software revenuesthe 81% gross margin business that justifies SaaS valuationsgrew just 9% year-over-year and represent only 32% of total revenue.

Compare this to Toast (TOST), which grew Q3 2025 revenue 25% to $1.63 billion while generating 35% adjusted EBITDA margins and 79% SaaS gross margins. Toast operates in the same restaurant POS market as Lightspeed but has achieved the profitability inflection that Lightspeed promises but doesn't deliver. Toast trades at 26x forward earnings; Lightspeed trades at 27x despite materially worse unit economics.

The bear case is straightforward: Lightspeed faces entrenched competitors (Shopify, Square, Clover, Toast) with deeper pockets and better margins in a market growing 13%+ annuallyyet Lightspeed's FY2026 guidance calls for just 12% growth. The company is losing market share while burning cash to fund a payments-led growth strategy that erodes gross margins and doesn't generate sufficient software attachment to justify the investment.

At current valuations (1.4x forward sales), shares price in modest expectations. But even these muted expectations may prove generous if software growth remains anemic and competition intensifies. Bearish view.

Operational Reality: Payments Penetration Driving Revenue, Not Software Expansion

Lightspeed operates a cloud-based POS and e-commerce platform serving retail, hospitality, and golf course operators across 100+ countries. The business model combines software subscriptions (high margin, recurring) with transaction-based payments processing (lower margin, volume-dependent).

Lightspeed reported total revenue of $312.3 million in Q3 FY2026, up 11% year-over-yeara deceleration from Q2's 15% growth. The composition tells the real story: subscription software grew just 6% to $93 million (30% of revenue), while transaction-based revenues increased 15% to $209.4 million (67% of revenue). Gross profit reached $133.6 million at a 43% margin, up from 42% in Q2 as operational efficiencies partially offset the revenue mix shift. The company posted adjusted EBITDA of $20.2 million (6% margin) but still reported a net loss of $33.6 million.

The divergence between software growth (6%) and transaction growth (15%) remains the core problem, though both have decelerated from Q2's 9% and 33% respectively. Payments penetration increased from 25% to 37% of GPV, which means more customers are processing transactions through Lightspeed but not necessarily buying more software features. Software gross margins remained strong at 81%, but that high-margin business is growing at less than half the rate of the overall market.

Gross margins contracted 200 basis points year-over-year as transaction revenues (29% margin) became a larger revenue mix. Software gross margins remained strong at 81%, but that high-margin business is growing at less than half the rate of the overall market. This is the inverse of what investors want to see in a SaaS businessyou want software (high margin, sticky, recurring) growing faster than services/transactions (low margin, commodity-like).

ARPU trends reveal the underlying dynamics: Consolidated ARPU reached $660 in Q3 FY2026, up from $145 in FY2022 but down slightly from Q2's $665. This growth is driven primarily by payments attach rather than software upsells. More notably, customer locations have reversed their declineLightspeed added approximately 2,600 net new locations in Q3 to reach ~148,000 total, representing 9% year-over-year growth in the company's two strategic growth engines (North American retail and European hospitality). This represents a meaningful shift from prior quarters where location count had been contracting, though it remains to be seen whether this growth proves sustainable or represents temporary stabilization.

The Toast Comparison: What Best-in-Class Execution Looks Like

Toast operates in Lightspeed's core restaurant vertical and provides a useful benchmark for what excellent execution produces in this market:

The numbers speak for themselves. Toast reported Q3 2025 revenue of $1.63 billion, up 25% year-over-year, while Lightspeed managed $319 million at 15% growth. More importantly, Toast generates 35% adjusted EBITDA marginsnearly six times Lightspeed's 6%. Toast added 7,500 net locations in the quarter while Lightspeed's location count has been declining for two years. The operational gap is wide and widening.

MetricToast (Q3 2025)Lightspeed (Q2 FY2026)
Revenue$1.63B (+25% YoY)$319M (+15% YoY)
Gross Margin26.5%42.4%
SaaS Gross Margin79%81%
Adj. EBITDA Margin35%6%
ARR$2.02B (+30% YoY)N/A
Net Location Adds7,500Declining
FCF Margin9.4%Negative

Toast is scaling profitably with 35% adjusted EBITDA margins while growing 25% annually. Lightspeed is delivering 6% adjusted EBITDA margins while growing 15%. Toast added 7,500 net locations in Q3; Lightspeed's location count has declined over the past two years.

The margin difference is particularly stark. Toast operates at lower gross margins (26.5% vs. 42.4%) because it sells hardware at/below cost as a customer acquisition tool, yet still generates 35% adjusted EBITDA margins through operational efficiency. Lightspeed operates asset-light with 42% gross margins but generates just 6% adjusted EBITDA margins because operating expenses remain elevated relative to revenue scale.

Toast's success demonstrates that the restaurant POS market offers substantial profit potential for well-executed businesses. Lightspeed's struggles suggest execution challenges rather than industry-wide headwinds.

Cash Burn and Balance Sheet: Runway Shortening

Lightspeed ended Q3 FY2026 with $479 million in cash, down from $659 million just one quarter earlier and $953 million in FY2022. The company has consumed approximately $474 million in cash over the past three years while revenues grew from $709 million (FY2022) to $1.22 billion (guided FY2026).

Operating cash flow showed improvement in Q3, generating $28.9 million compared to $2.7 million in the prior-year quarter. Adjusted free cash flow turned positive at $14.9 million for the quarter, representing the company's second consecutive quarter of positive free cash flow generation. On a trailing twelve-month basis, cash generation has improved materially from prior year levels, though the $180 million sequential cash decline from Q2 to Q3 suggests working capital fluctuations or one-time items affecting the quarterly comparison. At current cash levels of $479 million and improved operating cash flow trends, near-term liquidity concerns have diminished somewhat, though sustained cash generation remains to be proven.

The positive adjusted EBITDA of $32 million TTM sounds encouraging until you account for stock-based compensation ($34 million in the first half of FY2026 alone) and growth capex. Adjusting for these items, the business remains cash-consumptive rather than cash-generative. Management increased marketing spend by $10 million year-over-year in Q2, suggesting continued aggressive customer acquisition spending despite location count declines. This spend patternincreasing sales and marketing while losing net locationsindicates rising customer acquisition costs and elevated churn.

The positive adjusted EBITDA of $32 million TTM sounds encouraging until you account for stock-based compensation ($34 million in first half FY2026 alone) and growth capex. Adjusting for these items, the business remains cash-consumptive rather than cash-generative.

Management increased marketing spend by $10 million year-over-year in Q2 FY2026, suggesting continued aggressive customer acquisition spending despite location count declines. This spend patternincreasing sales and marketing while losing net locationsindicates rising customer acquisition costs and/or elevated churn.

For a company trading at $1.22 billion market cap with $479 million cash, the implied enterprise value of $801 million represents just 0.66x TTM revenue and 0.66x forward FY2026 guided revenue. This low valuation reflects the market's skepticism about path to sustained profitability and competitive positioning.

Competition and Market Share: Losing Ground in a Growing Market

The U.S. retail POS market is projected to grow 13.3% annually through 2030, with approximately 50% in retail, 30% in restaurants, and 20% in entertainment/other. Lightspeed's FY2026 guidance of 12% revenue growth implies the company is losing market share in an attractive, growing market.

The competitive landscape is daunting. Shopify dominates e-commerce POS with 22%+ expected revenue growth. Toast leads restaurant POS with 25% growth and $2 billion in ARR. Square maintains ubiquity in the SMB market through integrated payments and banking. Clover leverages massive distribution through bank partnerships. Each competitor has advantages Lightspeed lackswhether it's Shopify's merchant ecosystem, Toast's restaurant-specific execution, Square's brand recognition, or Clover's channel access.

Lightspeed's response has been vertical focus, targeting what CEO Dax Dasilva calls "complex, high-volume SMBs" in North American retail and European hospitality. The Q2 FY2026 earnings call emphasized wins with high-profile customers and European hospitality growth. But vertical specialization doesn't translate to sustainable competitive advantage when competitors like Toast in restaurants and Shopify in retail offer superior economics and product velocity. Product releases have focused on payments attachLightspeed Capital grew 121% year-over-year to $9.3 million quarterly revenuerather than software innovation that would justify premium pricing or drive software ARPU expansion.

Lightspeed's response has been vertical focustargeting "complex, high-volume SMBs" in specific verticals (North American retail, European hospitality) where CEO Dax Dasilva claims differentiated positioning. The Q2 FY2026 earnings call emphasized wins with high-profile customers and European hospitality growth.

The problem: vertical specialization doesn't translate to sustainable competitive advantage when competitors (Toast in restaurants, Shopify in retail) offer superior economics and product velocity. Lightspeed's product releases have focused on payments attach (Lightspeed Capital grew 121% YoY to $9.3 million quarterly revenue) rather than software innovation that would justify premium pricing or drive software ARPU expansion.

Management's commentary about "cost of growth" rising due to competition is telling. When asked about competitive dynamics, CEO Dasilva acknowledged intense competition but didn't articulate clear differentiation beyond "hyper focus on our particular verticals." This suggests Lightspeed is competing primarily on service and vertical knowledge rather than technological or economic moats.

Valuation: Modest Expectations Already Priced In

At $9.34 per share ($1.28B market cap, $801M enterprise value), Lightspeed trades at 0.66x TTM revenue, 0.66x forward revenue, and 21x forward earnings. Running three scenarios shows how the risk/reward skews:

The bull case requires software growth to reaccelerate substantially and margins to expand 400 basis pointsboth counter to current trends. The base case offers minimal upside. The bear case becomes plausible if competition intensifies or payments penetration stalls.

For investors seeking 15%+ annual returns, Lightspeed would need to reach $12+ within 12-18 months, requiring either multiple expansion (from 27x to 35x+ forward earnings) or materially better-than-guided results. Neither seems likely given competitive dynamics and execution track record.

What Would Make This Investable?

Three developments would shift this from sell to hold or buy, but none appear imminent.

Software growth would need to reaccelerate to 15%+ annually. If subscription revenuecurrently $93 million quarterly and growing just 6% year-over-yearaccelerated to $110+ million quarterly, it would signal Lightspeed is successfully upselling existing customers and winning software-first deals rather than payments-led relationships. This would improve gross margins and justify higher valuation multiples. Q3 results showed subscription growth decelerating further to 6%, and with full-year FY2026 revenue guidance of $1.216-1.220 billion implying modest Q4 growth, and price increases implemented in mid-2025 haven't moved the needle. If software growth remains sub-10% through Q4 FY2026, the thesis deteriorates further.

EBITDA margins would need to expand to 15%+ within twelve months. If Lightspeed demonstrated operational leverage by growing margins from 6% to 15% while maintaining revenue growth, it would validate that the business model works at scale. This would require materially reducing sales and marketing spend as a percentage of revenue or achieving better gross margins through software mix shift. Given that management just increased marketing spend $10 million year-over-year, margin expansion through efficiency seems unlikely. The path to 15% EBITDA margins requires either software revenue mix shifting to 45%+ of total (from 32% currently) or transaction gross margins improving substantiallyneither appears imminent.

The company would need to demonstrate durable competitive advantages. If Lightspeed articulated and showed clear competitive moatssuperior retention, pricing power, technological differentiationthat justified confidence in long-term market share gains, the investment case would strengthen. Currently, the company competes primarily on vertical expertise and service quality, neither of which creates sustainable advantages against well-capitalized competitors. Watch for customer retention rates stabilizing, ARPU expansion driven by software rather than payments, improving win rates against Toast and Shopify, and gross margins stabilizing despite payments growth.

Investment Conclusion: Avoid Until Software Momentum Returns

Lightspeed Commerce operates in an attractive, growing market (13%+ CAGR through 2030) but is losing market share (guiding 12% growth) while competing against better-capitalized, more profitable peers. The company's payments-led growth strategy is driving revenue but eroding gross margins and failing to generate commensurate software attachment.

The comparison to Toast is particularly damning. Toast demonstrates that restaurant POS can generate 35% EBITDA margins at scale; Lightspeed generates 6%. Toast adds 7,500 net locations quarterly; Lightspeed's location count is declining. Toast trades at a premium because it earns it; Lightspeed trades at a discount because competitive positioning is deteriorating.

At $9.34 per share (0.66x revenue, 21x forward earnings), the valuation appears optically cheap but reflects realistic expectations for a business with structural challenges. The base case offers minimal upside; the bear case involves significant downside if software growth remains anemic or competition intensifies.

For investors seeking growth in the POS/payments space, Toast offers superior risk-adjusted returns despite trading at higher multiples. For value investors attracted to Lightspeed's depressed valuation, the risk is not that shares are cheap but that they're cheap for good reasonand may get cheaper if execution doesn't improve materially.

Rating: Bearish. Avoid until software revenue demonstrates sustained 15%+ growth or EBITDA margins expand to 12-15%, neither of which appears likely over the next 12-18 months.

data.title: Markets are getting closer to a buying opportunity as investors capitulate, says Bank of America strategist
data.description:
By Jules Rimmer

Global markets need to correct another 3% to 5% to indicate a major buying opportunity

For the U.S. voter, investor, politician and consumer, the price of gas is all-important

The market hasn't capitulated, but it's getting closer.

The best signal to add risk will be when 88% of global equity indices are trading below their 50- and 200-day moving average, say strategists at Bank of America led by Michael Hartnett.

Right now, the S&P 500 SPX is there but global markets need to drop another 3% to 5% to trigger the big buying opportunity.

One other indicator that the time is right to buy might be if the cash weightings in investor portfolios rises to 5%. Bank of America's fund manager survey for March showed a rise from a 2026 low of 3.2% to 4.2% so that could easily materialize.

Losses are mount as oil prices surge - the Brent contract is up by two thirds this year due to the war in Iran and attacks on energy infrastructure throughout the region.

Trump...Wall St versus Main St... S&P 500 and Trump economic approval rating

Hartnett assumes the looming midterm elections in November may persuade President Donald Trump to de-escalate as soon as he can and this is the chief motivation behind Bank of America's key calls to sell the dollar index DXY above 100, buy the long bond BX:TMUBMUSD30Y at 5% yield and go long the S&P 500 if it dips below 6600, which it very nearly did on Thursday.

If the war ends without any bounce in Trump's popularity, though, Hartnett warns that there may not be any new summer highs. The chart above illustrates the link between Trump and stocks.

The correction that accelerated this month really started in October, when the Fed was cutting rates with stock markets at their highs. "Big corrections," Hartnett says, "end when sell-off leaders are so oversold they trough."

That's happening now in bitcoin (BTCUSD), software IGV, and the Magnificent Seven MAGS. There's also been a painful capitulation on overbought longs like gold (GC00), precious metals, semiconductors SOXX and emerging markets EEM.

Once markets believe that oil is back below $100 for good, Hartnett and team think it will be much safer for investors to add risk back.

Hartnett identified three key investment themes for the second half of the 2020s. First, the commodity bull market is broadening out from gold to metals and energy, so those nations in control of strategically important chips, rare earths, minerals and oil supplies will have a major advantage.

Second, Bank of America forecasts investors to prefer international and U.S mid-cap stocks as opposed to over-leveraged U.S. large-caps.

Finally Hartnett and team recommend exposure to contrarian consumer stocks that may benefit from populist and socialist policies designed to favor the lower-income electorate.

-Jules Rimmer

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: FedEx's Earnings Quality Is Improving — Market Talk
data.description: Truist analysts Lucas Servera and Deniel Ighodaro are feeling incrementally more bullish about FedEx after its F3Q report, due to the shipping company's yield strength, volume growth and continued cost execution. "Importantly, demand trends appear stable into 4Q, while mix is shifting toward higher-margin B2B," they say in a research note. "With guidance raised and earnings quality improving (in our view), we have increased confidence in a multi-lever recovery story supporting further multiple expansion." Truist maintains FedEx's buy rating and raises its price target to $425 from $400. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Federal Student Assistance Partnership Announced
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - The U.S. Department of the Treasury and the U.S. Department of Education have announced the Federal Student Assistance Partnership to enhance the administration of Federal student aid programs, mitigate the continuing fallout and cost to taxpayers from the previous Administration's mismanagement of the Federal student loan portfolio, and facilitate the return of defaulted borrowers to repayment.

Currently, ED's student loan portfolio stands at nearly $1.7 trillion with fewer than 40 percent of borrowers in repayment and almost 25 percent of borrowers in default. Student loan debt is roughly twice the size of all American university endowments combined and is larger than either the nation's cumulative credit card debt or cumulative auto debt, according to the Treasury. ED was never intended to operate what would be the fifth-largest commercial bank in the United States, distributing over $100 billion each year in Federal student loans and grants, it added.

'The Federal Student Assistance Partnership marks an intentional and historic step toward breaking up the Federal education bureaucracy and dramatically improving the administration of Federal student aid programs that millions of American students, families, and borrowers rely on to access higher education,' said U.S. Secretary of Education Linda McMahon. 'As the Federal student aid portfolio soars to nearly $1.7 trillion and with nearly a quarter of student loan borrowers in default, Americans know that the Department of Education has failed to effectively manage and deliver these critical programs. By leveraging Treasury's world-renowned expertise in finance and economic policy, we are confident that American students, borrowers, and taxpayers will finally have functioning programs after decades of mismanagement.'

Under the new interagency agreement, Treasury will assume operational responsibility for collecting on defaulted Federal student loan debt and provide operational support to ED's efforts to return borrowers to repayment. In subsequent phases, Treasury will work to provide operational support over non-defaulted Federal student loan debt, to the extent practicable and permitted by law, while also seeking opportunities to provide operational support to FSA's other functions.

'Under President Trump's leadership we are undertaking the first serious effort to clean up a $1.7 trillion portfolio that has been badly mismanaged for years. Treasury has the unique experience, the operational capability, and the financial expertise to bring long overdue financial discipline to the program and be better stewards of taxpayer dollars,' said U.S. Secretary of the Treasury Scott Bessent.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Youth Much Less Happy In North America, Western Europe: World Happiness Report
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - In North America and Western Europe, young people are much less happy than 15 years ago. Over the same period, social media use has greatly increased. Many people blame social media for this fall in happiness, according to findings published in the 2026 edition of the World Happiness Report.

Heavy social media use appears to be contributing to the drop in well-being among young people in English-speaking countries and Western Europe, especially among girls, says the Report, published on Thursday by the Wellbeing Research Center at the University of Oxford, in partnership with Gallup, the UN Sustainable Development Solutions Network and an independent editorial board.

Life evaluations among under 25s in the United States, Canada, Australia and New Zealand have dropped dramatically over the past decade, while the average for the young in the rest of the world has increased, according to Gallup World Poll data. Despite similar levels of social media use compared to other countries, the largest drops in well-being among young people are observed in these countries.

One international survey of 15-year-olds in nearly 50 countries suggests that heavy social media use is associated with a significant drop in well-being among the students surveyed.

Young people who use social media for less than one hour per day report the highest levels of well-being - higher than those who do not use social media at all. But adolescents are, by one estimate, spending an average of 2.5 hours a day on social media.

The findings are published ahead of the UN's International Day of Happiness.

The 14th edition of the World Happiness Report also contains a ranking of the world's happiest countries.

Finland leads the world in happiness for a record ninth year in a row, with Finns reporting an average score of 7.764 (out of 10) when asked to evaluate their lives.

Costa Rica climbs to its best-ever position, continuing a multi-year rise from a low of 23rd in 2023, to 4th this year, while former table-topper Switzerland re-enters the top 10 after a one-year absence.

Continued upward trends for countries such as Kosovo (16th), Slovenia (18th) and Czechia (20th) underline the convergence of happiness levels between Central and Eastern Europe, and Western Europe.

The 2026 rankings mark the second year in a row that none of the English-speaking countries, New Zealand (11th), Ireland (13th), Australia (15th), United States (23rd), Canada (25th), and the UK (29th) appear in the top 10. Nations in or near zones of major conflict remain at the fag end of the rankings.

Jan-Emmanuel De Neve, Director of Oxford's Wellbeing Research Center, Professor of Economics at the University of Oxford, and an editor of the World Happiness Report, said: 'The global evidence makes clear that the links between social media use and our well-being heavily depend on what platforms we're using, who's using them and how, as well as for how long. Heavy usage is associated with much lower well-being, but those deliberately off social media also appear to be missing out on some positive effects. Beyond the complexity, it is clear that we should look as much as possible to put the 'social' back into social media.'

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Bitcoin RSI eyes 2022 repeat as analysis suggests 'time to pay attention'
data.description:
Bitcoin is hinting at its next long-term bottom as a key leading indicator preps a higher low.

Key points:

  • Bitcoin RSI is approaching a critical long-term position for the fate of the bear market.

  • RSI needs a weekly bullish divergence to repeat its early-2023 rebound.

  • A trader says he is “not in a rush” to reenter the market with the comedown from all-time highs just a few months old.

Bitcoin RSI: All eyes on higher low

New analysis covering relative strength index (RSI) data on concludes it could soon be “time to pay attention.”

Bitcoin bear-market bottoms often follow the start of a bullish divergence with RSI on weekly time frames.

For trader Jelle, current market behavior is following historical trends, and Bitcoin’s next inflection point may be around the corner.

“When $BTC's weekly RSI makes a higher low again, it's time to pay attention,” he wrote on X.

A classic bullish divergence locks in when RSI makes a higher low while price makes lower lows. Jelle, however, says that price has room to maneuver and still preserve the emerging recovery.

“Doesn't matter if BTC makes a higher low, equal low, or lower low,” he continued. 

“When RSI starts moving higher again, the bottom is very close - or already in.”

BTC price bear flag still in play

RSI last flipped bullish at the end of Bitcoin’s 2022 bear market, and its signals preceded a period of upside that continued for over a year.

At the time, talk also focused on reclaiming the 200-week exponential moving average (EMA) as support, something that occurred in March 2023. 

As Cointelegraph reported, the 200-week EMA was only lost again last month, with analysis calling the trend line “unreliable.” 

Jelle, meanwhile, is among those speculating that previous cycles demand a much longer bear market than the few months that have elapsed so far.

“Previous bear markets all lasted around a year. $BTC topped just 23 weeks ago, and looks like this,” he told X followers. 

“I'm not in a rush to buy back in.”

A separate chart drew attention to a possible bear flag formation under development — a sign of weakness that could result in a fresh support failure in a manner similar to January.

data.title: ICE Canola Looking to Add Gains
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Canola futures on the Intercontinental Exchange were attempting to climb higher Friday morning, fending off a bout of choppiness.

Support came from gains in Chicago soybeans and soyoil, Malaysian palm oil and MATIF rapeseed, while Chicago soymeal was lower. Crude oil was virtually unchanged as it moved away from earlier declines.

The May canola contract remained handily above its major moving averages, underpinning the oilseed. Canola crush margins pushed higher, with the May position nearly at C$290 per metric ton above the averages.

At 292,100 metric tons, canola exports increased more than 157% during the week ended March 15, the Canadian Grain Commission reported. That brought the cumulative total to 4.88 million metric tons versus 6.52 million the same time last year.

The Canadian dollar was higher Friday morning with the loonie at 72.94 U.S. cents compared with Thursday's close of 72.84.

Approximately 12,900 contracts had been traded by 9:40 EDT and prices in Canadian dollars per metric ton were:

 
           Price      Change 
May       730.50     up 2.20 
Jul       742.90     up 1.80 
Nov       734.30     up 0.50 
Jan       738.30     up 0.40

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com

data.title: FedEx Signals Confidence in Earnings Trajectory — Market Talk
data.description: FedEx reports F3Q results substantially ahead of expectations, Stifel analysts say in a research note. The beat was fueled by higher revenue per package both domestically and internationally, and by a higher volume of packages in the U.S. The shipping company is also benefiting from cost cutting related to the company's ongoing turnaround plan, the analysts say, as it prepares to spin off its Freight business in June. FedEx additionally raises its outlook. "While the increase mostly captures this quarter's beat, we think it was also a healthy indication of confidence in the earnings trajectory," the analysts write. "Middle East conflict seems less of a concern than we'd anticipated, but is something we'll monitor closely." Shares rise 2.4%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Fed's Waller says he doesn't support rate hikes, sees inflation cooling in the second half of the year
data.description:
The Federal Reserve doesn't need to raise interest rates because inflation is likely to cool in the second half of the year, said Federal Reserve governor Christopher Waller.

Waller said that inflation was high but didn't get worse last year after the Trump White House enacted tariffs. That tells him that underlying inflation likely improved as tariffs pushed prices higher.

"I think once we get past tariffs and maybe the second quarter, you're going to see inflation come back down. I don't think there's a need for rate hikes," Waller said in an interview on CNBC.

At the Fed meeting this week, Waller supported no change in interest rates. He said after the weak February jobs report he was going to advocate for a cut but then he decided to be cautious given the outlook for the war with Iran was looking like it would be more protracted.

If the economy can weather the storm from the war, Waller said, he would return to advocating for rate cuts later this year.

-Greg Robb

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Iran War Prompts Caution From a Fed Dove — WSJ
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

Fed governor Christopher Waller has supported rate cuts for most of the past year and dissented against the central bank's decision to hold rates steady in January. He was ready to dissent again this week, he told CNBC on Friday morning-but the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the potentially inflationary effects of a global oil crunch led him to vote for a pause instead.

"Two weeks ago, when the jobs report came out and it was negative 92,000, I thought, that's it. I'm dissenting," Waller said in the TV interview. "Since that time, the Strait of Hormuz was closed, this is looking like it's going to be a much more protracted conflict, and oil prices are going to stay high for a long time."

Waller's comments show how anxiety about the inflationary effects of the war helped Chair Jerome Powell build an 11-1 majority in favor of holding rates steady this week. Over the past year, Waller has resisted the argument that President Trump's tariffs are likely to lead to sustained inflation, but he warned Friday that an oil shock could be different.

"Oil is a major intermediate import, and it will at some point bleed through," he said.

Earlier Friday, another dovish Fed official who also supported this week's pause, Michelle Bowman, said in a separate TV interview that she still believes weaker hiring could prompt three Fed rate cuts this year.

"I'm still concerned about the job market," she said.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).
